title: Elevated error rate after deploy
summary: Steps to diagnose and roll back when error rates spike shortly after a deployment
steps: Confirm timing: check if the error rate spike began within 15 minutes of a deploy. If yes, the deploy is the prime suspect.
Check the deploy changelog: what changed? Look for anything touching error-prone paths (payment, auth, DB writes).
Sample the errors: pull a few error messages from logs. Are they all the same class of error, or varied?
If errors are concentrated in new code paths from this deploy → proceed to rollback.
Initiate rollback: deploy the previous version. Note the version you're rolling back to.
Monitor for 10 minutes post-rollback. If error rate recovers, confirm root cause was the deploy and file an incident report.
If error rate does NOT recover after rollback, the deploy may be a red herring — escalate and check for infrastructure issues (DB, upstream dependencies).

title: Database connection exhaustion
summary: When services can't get DB connections from the pool
steps: Check active connection count on the DB primary (dashboard: 'PG Connections').
If near the connection limit: identify which service is holding the most connections.
Check for connection leaks: a recent deploy that forgot to release connections is the usual culprit.
Short-term mitigation: increase pool size or restart the offending service to release leaked connections.
Long-term: file a bug against the leaking code path.

title: Upstream dependency degradation
summary: When an external service we depend on (payment processor, auth provider) is slow or erroring
steps: Check the provider's status page for acknowledged incidents.
Verify our requests are actually reaching them (vs. failing before egress).
If provider is down and we have a fallback/circuit breaker, confirm it's engaged.
If no fallback: communicate to customer-facing teams that the issue is upstream. Set expectations.
Monitor provider status. Resume normal operation once they recover.

title: Memory pressure / OOM kills
summary: When pods are being killed for exceeding memory limits
steps: Identify which pods are being OOM-killed (kubectl get events, filter for OOMKilled).
Check if memory usage has been trending up (slow leak) or spiked suddenly (load-driven).
If slow leak: likely a code issue. Restart buys time, but file a bug.
If load spike: check if traffic is genuinely elevated. May need to scale horizontally.
Short-term: bump memory limits by 25% to stop the bleeding while investigating.
Do NOT just keep bumping limits — find the root cause.
